wikidata_org_candidates.label: AIL Systems Inc
wikidata_org_candidates.reasoning: The employer 'Airborne Instruments' is a common shortened name for 'Airborne Instruments Laboratory' (AIL). The Wikidata entity Q128831578 represents AIL Systems Inc, which was founded as Airborne Instruments Laboratory in 1945. The location mentioned on the card (Melville, NY) is adjacent to Deer Park, NY (the headquarters listed in Wikidata), both of which are on Long Island where AIL was historically based. Furthermore, the employee's field of microwave antenna design matches the company's known specialization in radar and aerospace electronics.

results.possibleIdentity: Harold Moss was an electrical engineer active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, primarily associated with Airborne Instruments Laboratory (AIL) in Melville, New York. He specialized in microwave antenna design and electronic control systems. Moss is historically recognized for his role as a technical collaborator with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which fostered partnerships between the engineering community and contemporary artists. Notably, Moss was one of the lead engineers from AIL who provided technical assistance for Robert Rauschenberg's 1968 kinetic environment "Solstice," which utilized complex electronic systems, including photoelectric cells and motorized panels, to respond to the movement of viewers. His work with AIL and E.A.T. placed him at the center of the technological and artistic intersection in New York during that era.
